name: The Mice
description: These colliding galaxies are nicknamed "The Mice" because of the long tails of stars and gas emanating from each galaxy. The pair, also known as NGC 4676, will eventually merge. This image was among the first released from Hubble's newly installed Advanced Camera for Surveys in 2002.

name: Galaxy C35
description: The giant elliptical galaxy Caldwell 35, also known as NGC 4889, is about two and a half times larger than the Milky Way. Hidden in the heart of this tranquil-seeming galaxy lies a supermassive black hole with a mass 21 billion times greater than the Sun.

name: Black Eye Galaxy (M64)
description: Easily identified by the spectacular band of absorbing dust partially obscuring its bright nucleus, the Black Eye galaxy, also called Messier 64 or NGC 4374, is speckled with hot blue stars that have just formed, as well as pink clouds of glowing hydrogen.

name: Needle Galaxy (C38)
description: Caldwell 38, or NGC 4565, is nicknamed the Needle galaxy, because in most telescopes it looks as thin and sharp as a pin. It is a spiral galaxy, but because it is seen almost perfectly edge-on, its spiral structure is concealed.

name: Galaxy C36
description: This image captures the core and some of the arms of the spiral galaxy Caldwell 36, also known as NGC 4559, located roughly 30 million light-years from Earth.

name: Dwarf Galaxy NGC 4163
description: This swarm of stars is the dwarf galaxy NGC 4163, located 10 million light-years from Earth in the constellation Canes Venatici. Irregularly shaped red blobs are regions of active star formation.

name: Dwarf Galaxy NGC 4214
description: The dwarf galaxy NGC 4214 is ablaze with young stars and gas clouds. This image captures intricate patterns of glowing hydrogen shaped during the star-birthing process, cavities blown clear of gas by stellar winds, and bright stellar clusters.

name: Cat's Eye Galaxy (M94)
description: New stars are forming at a high rate within the bright starburst ring of spiral galaxy Messier 94, or NGC 4736. Although M94 was initially believed to be roughly 30,000 light-years in diameter, two faint arms (not shown) were discovered extending far into space, tripling its diameter.

name: Galaxy C29
description: Caldwell 29, also known as NGC 5005, is a spiral galaxy located roughly 75 million light-years from Earth. It likely harbors a supermassive black hole at its heart.

name: Star Cluster M3
description: Globular cluster Messier 3, or NGC 5272, is known to contain over 500,000 stars, and more variable stars ― 274 ― than any other cluster. Certain variable stars are known to brighten and dim in a predictable way over time, allowing astronomers to use these stars to calculate cosmic distances.

name: Star Cluster M53
description: Thousands and thousands of brilliant stars make up the globular cluster Messier 53, also called NGC 5024. With its stars bound tightly by gravity, the cluster is roughly spherical and becomes denser toward its heart.

name: Galaxy NGC 4710
description: The magnificent galaxy NGC 4710 is tilted nearly edge-on to our view from Earth. This perspective allows the central bulge of stars to be easily distinguished from the galaxy's pancake-flat disk of stars, dust, and gas.

name: Quasar QSO J1244
description: In the center of this image is quasar QSO J1244+1721, seemingly topped by a faint elliptical galaxy. Although the two objects appear to be close to each other, they are actually separated by about 2 billion light-years. This was Hubble's 100,000th exposure, captured June 22, 1996.

name: Galaxy M91
description: The spiral galaxy Messier 91, or NGC 4548, is one of over a thousand galaxies that make up the Virgo cluster — a group of galaxies that are gravitationally bound to one another. It is an anemic galaxy, with a lower rate of star formation compared to other spiral galaxies.

name: Galaxies NGC 4302 and NGC 4298
description: This image captures two spiral galaxies. They look quite different because we see them from different angles. The edge-on galaxy (bottom) is called NGC 4302, and the other is NGC 4298.

name: Galaxy M85
description: Messier 85, also called NGC 4382, is either an elliptical or a lenticular galaxy. Lenticular galaxies have qualities of both elliptical and spiral galaxies and are sometimes called armless spirals.

name: Galaxy NGC 4150
description: This image captures the ancient ellipitical galaxy NGC 4150, located about 44 million light-years away. It shows streamers of dust and gas wrapped around the galaxy's core. Closer views of the core reveal clumps of young, blue stars less than a billion years old.

name: Galaxy NGC 4013
description: A dark band of dust bisects the spiral galaxy NGC 4013. This edge-on galaxy is located 55 million light-years away in the constellation Ursa Major.

name: Galaxy M106
description: This image of Messier 106, also called NGC 4258, reveals one of the spiral galaxy's most striking features: its extra pair of arms. Most spiral galaxies only have one pair of arms, but M106 has an extra set made of hot gas, seen here as red wisps.

name: Sunflower Galaxy (M63)
description: The Sunflower galaxy, also called Messier 63 or NGC 5055, is a flocculent spiral galaxy, which means it lacks well-defined arms. Although it only has two arms, many more appear to be winding around its yellow core.

name: Galaxy C45
description: Caldwell 45, or NGC 5248, is a spiral galaxy notable for the ring structure around its nucleus. These nuclear rings are characterized by “hot spots” of starburst activity, where stars form at a much higher rate than usual.

name: Galaxy M60
description: This image captures elliptical galaxy Messier 60 (center) along with the bluish spiral galaxy NGC 4647 (upper right). NGC 4647 is about two-thirds the size of M60, which is also known as NGC 4649, and is much less massive. The two galaxies form a pair known as Arp 116.

name: Galaxy M89
description: Elliptical galaxy Messier 89, or NGC 4552, is almost exactly circular. It contains about 100 billion stars and well over 2,000 globular clusters. Its high population of stars and clusters might be why the galaxy has a larger region of light surrounding it than other elliptical galaxies.

name: Galaxy M99
description: This image shows a detailed view of the spiral arms on one side of the galaxy Messier 99, or NGC 4254. This galaxy is called a grand-design spiral, with long, large, and clearly defined spiral arms.

name: Galaxy M98
description: Messier 98, or NGC 4192, is a galaxy of about a trillion stars as well as an abundance of neutral hydrogen gas and interstellar dust. Because of the high amounts of gas and dust, it has numerous star-forming regions, especially in its nucleus and arms.

name: Lensed Star Icarus
description: Icarus, whose official name is MACS J1149+2223 Lensed Star 1, is the farthest individual star ever seen. It is only visible because it is being magnified by the gravity of a massive galaxy cluster, located about 5 billion light-years from Earth, called MACS J1149+2223.

name: Dwarf Galaxy Kiso 5639
description: Hubble captured a firestorm of star birth lighting up one end of this dwarf galaxy. Called Kiso 5639, it is a member of a class of "tadpole" galaxies so named because of their bright heads and elongated tails.

name: Galaxies Arp 148
description: Arp 148 is the aftermath of an encounter between two galaxies, resulting in a ring-shaped galaxy and an elongated companion. The shapes and arrangement of the galaxies suggest that this is a snapshot of an ongoing collision.

name: Galaxy NGC 3949
description: Like our Milky Way, the galaxy NGC 3949 has a disk full of young, blue stars peppered with pink star-birth regions. In contrast to the blue disk, the galaxy's bright center is made up of mostly older stars and appears more yellow.

name: Galaxy M109
description: This image shows the core of Messier 109, a barred spiral galaxy and the brightest member of a group of 79 galaxies in the constellation Ursa Major. Interactions with three companion galaxies may contribute to its distinctive structure.

name: Galaxies Arp 297
description: Arp 297 is a pair of interacting galaxies that consists of NGC 5754, the large spiral at the top, and NGC 5752, the smaller companion at the bottom left. NGC 5754's internal structure has hardly been disturbed, but it does have some kinked arms just beyond its inner ring.

name: Einstein Ring SDSS J125028.25+052349
description: Einstein rings like this object, designated SDSS J125028.25+052349, form when two galaxies are almost perfectly aligned, one behind the other, and the gravitational field of the closer galaxy bends the light from the more-distant galaxy into bright arcs around itself.

name: Quasar 3C 273
description: The quasar 3C 273 resides at the heart of a galaxy nearly 2 billion light-years away. A quasar is a brilliant source of energy at the center of a distant galaxy, and is believed to flare up when gas, dust, or other material falls onto a supermassive black hole at the galaxy's core.

name: Galaxy NGC 4261 Core
description: In 1992 Hubble investigated the supermassive black hole in galaxy NGC 4261. This debris disk, which is 300 light-years across, surrounds the black hole. Material is heated to millions of degrees as it is pulled toward the black hole.

name: Exoplanet K2-18b
description: This artist's depiction shows planet K2-18b orbiting a red dwarf star about 110 light-years away. Hubble made the first detection of water vapor in the atmosphere of this exoplanet, which lies within its star's “habitable zone,” where temperatures are right for liquid water to exist on the surface.

name: Galaxy M66
description: Spiral galaxy Messier 66 is a member of the Leo Triplet of galaxies. M66, or NGC 3627, displays asymmetric spiral arms and a core that appears to be off center — features likely caused by the gravitational pull of the other two galaxies in the group.

name: Galaxy C40
description: This image of Caldwell 40, or NGC 3626, shows off the spiral galaxy's galactic bulge, a densely packed region of stars that encompasses the heart of the galaxy.

name: Galaxy NGC 3310
description: There are several hundred star clusters in the starburst galaxy NGC 3310. They appear in this image as the bright, blue clumps that trace the galaxy's spiral arms.

name: Surfboard Galaxy (M108)
description: The Surfboard galaxy, also called Messier 108 or NGC 3556, shows little evidence of a well-defined spiral pattern, but it is classified as a barred spiral galaxy with loosely wound spiral arms. Observations show young star clusters exposed against a mottled and detail-rich background.

name: Galaxies UGC 06471 and UGC 06472
description: This image shows a cosmic collision between two galaxies, UGC 06471 and UGC 06472. The colliding galaxies are 145 million light-years from Earth. Such collisions distort the shapes of the galaxies as they merge and eventually form a larger galaxy.

name: GOODS-North Field
description: This image captures about 15,000 galaxies stretching back through 11 billion years of cosmic history. Hubble examined this part of the sky, located near the Big Dipper and called the GOODS-North field, as part of the Great Observatories Origins Deep Survey (GOODS).

name: Galaxy GN-z11
description: The farthest galaxy yet seen, GN-z11, appears in Hubble's view as it was 13.4 billion years in the past. This surprisingly bright, infant galaxy is seen as it existed just 400 million years after the big bang, when the universe was only three percent of its current age.

name: Pinwheel Galaxy (M101)
description: The Pinwheel galaxy, or Messier 101, has a pancake-like shape that we view face-on. This perspective shows off the spiral structure that gives the galaxy its nickname.

name: Galaxy EGS-ZS8-1
description: This extremely distant galaxy (inset), identified in a field of galaxies in the Cosmic Assembly Near-infrared Deep Extragalactic Legacy Survey (CANDELS), existed over 13 billion years ago. It is one of the most distant confirmed galaxies known.

name: Supernova Didius
description: Galaxy cluster RXJ1532.9+3021 magnifies the light of the very distant Supernova Didius, the white dot in the center of this image. The bright blob at upper left is the core of the supernova's host galaxy. The supernova is so far away, we see it as it appeared 7 billion years ago.

name: Hoag's Object
description: A nearly perfect ring of hot, blue stars pinwheels about the yellow nucleus of an unusual galaxy known as Hoag's Object. Curiously, a background object that bears an uncanny resemblance to Hoag's Object can be seen in the gap at the one o'clock position.

name: Galaxies Arp 274
description: Arp 274 is a system of three galaxies that appear to be partially overlapping. Two of the galaxies are rapidly forming new stars, evident in the bright blue knots strung along the arms of the galaxy on the right and along the small galaxy on the left.

name: Galaxy NGC 5584
description: The brilliant, blue glow of young stars traces the graceful spiral arms of galaxy NGC 5584. Thin, dark dust lanes appear to be flowing from the yellowish core, where older stars reside.

name: Galaxy Cluster Abell 1689
description: This image shows the inner region of Abell 1689, an immense cluster of galaxies located 2.2 billion light-years away. Astronomers used Hubble to map the distrubition of dark matter in the galaxy cluster.

name: Galaxy C52
description: Caldwell 52 is an elliptical galaxy and the most prominent member of a collection of galaxies known as the NGC 4697 Group. Slightly larger than the Andromeda galaxy, this massive elliptical dominates its group, distorting its neighbors with its gravitational influence.

name: Galaxy Leo IV
description: This image shows some of the stars of Leo IV, one of over a dozen ultra-faint dwarf galaxies near the Milky Way. These galaxies are dominated by dark matter, an invisible substance that makes up most of the universe's mass. Leo IV's stars are almost lost against a tapestry of background galaxies.

name: Galaxy M96
description: Dust and gas are unevenly spread throughout Messier 96's weak spiral arms, which are asymmetrical because of its gravitational interaction with neighboring galaxies. M96, or NGC 3368, is also remarkable for the location of its core, which is not exactly at the galactic center.

name: Galaxy M105
description: Like most elliptical galaxies, Messier 105, or NGC 3379, appears rather featureless and inactive. However, Hubble revealed young stars and star clusters in M105, indicating that star formation is still taking place.

name: Galaxy Cluster SDSS J1004+4112
description: This picture captures a galaxy cluster called SDSS J1004+4112 that's so massive that its gravity bends light from galaxies behind it. The light of a distant quasar (the brilliant core of an active galaxy) has been bent around the cluster, appearing in five places in this image.

name: Galaxy NGC 3079
description: This image reveals the dramatic activities within the core of the galaxy NGC 3079, where a bubble of hot gas is rising from a cauldron of glowing material. The structure is more than 3,000 light-years wide and rises 3,500 light-years above the galaxy's disk.

name: Galaxy C3
description: Caldwell 3, also known as NGC 4236, is one of at least 34 gravitationally bound galaxies in the Ursa Major galaxy group. It is a barred spiral galaxy, possessing an elongated lane of stars that cuts through its center.

name: Spindle Galaxy (M102)
description: Hubble's sharp vision reveals a crisp dust lane dividing the Spindle galaxy, also known as Messier 102, into two halves. This edge-on view of M102, also called NGC 5866, highlights the galaxy's structure.

name: Galaxies Arp 220
description: Arp 220 is the result of a collision between two spiral galaxies that began 700 million years ago. Located about 250 million light-years from Earth, it is one of the nearest galaxy mergers to our planet.

name: Seyfert's Sextet
description: At first, Seyfert's Sextet looks like six galaxies grouped closely together. However, the small galaxy with the prominent spiral arms (right of center) is much farther away than the others, and a bright clump to the lower right is material torn from one of the other galaxies.

name: Sombrero Galaxy (M104)
description: The Sombrero galaxy's hallmark is a brilliant white core encircled by thick lanes of dust. As seen from Earth, the spiral galaxy ― also called Messier 104 or NGC 4594 ― is tilted nearly edge-on.

name: Dwarf Galaxy UGC 5340
description: This image captures the dwarf galaxy UGC 5340. A pocket of rapid star birth appears in the lower right corner. This region of star formation was probably triggered by a gravitational interaction with an unseen companion galaxy.

name: Hanny's Voorwerp
description: This image shows a ghostly green blob of gas that appears to float near a normal-looking spiral galaxy. The bizarre object, dubbed Hanny's Voorwerp, is the visible part of a 300,000-light-year-long streamer of gas stretching around the galaxy, called IC 2497.

name: Galaxy NGC 2841
description: Young, blue stars and dark lanes of dust trace the winding arms of NGC 2841. Winds from the young stars may have cleared out the gas needed for additional star birth and halted star formation in the spiral galaxy.

name: Dwarf Galaxy I Zwicky 18
description: This image captures the irregular dwarf galaxy I Zwicky 18 and a companion galaxy to its upper right. The two galaxies are interacting, triggering star formation in I Zwicky 18.

name: Galaxy Holmberg IX
description: This loose collection of stars is actually a dwarf irregular galaxy, called Holmberg IX. Of the more than 20,000 stars that can be resolved in this image, only about 10 percent are considered to be old stars.

name: Galaxies NGC 6090
description: NGC 6090 is a pair of spiral galaxies with overlapping central regions and two long tidal tails made of material ripped out of the galaxies by gravitational interactions. The two visible cores are approximately 10,000 light-years apart.

name: Hercules Cluster (M13)
description: Like shiny flakes sparkling in a snow globe, over 100,000 stars whirl within the globular cluster Messier 13, also called NGC 6205, one of the brightest star clusters visible from the Northern Hemisphere.

name: Galaxies Arp 272
description: Arp 272 is a collision between two spiral galaxies, linked by their swirling arms. The galaxies, also called NGC 6050, are members of the Hercules Galaxy cluster.

name: Star Cluster M5
description: This image of globular cluster Messier 5, or NGC 5904, features over 100,000 stars. A majority formed more than 12 billion years ago, but there are some unexpected young stars on the scene ― perhaps created by collisions and interactions between stars in the packed cluster.

name: Galaxy Cluster TN J1338-1942
description: The galaxy cluster TN J1338-1942 contains a massive embryonic galaxy surrounded by smaller developing galaxies. The central galaxy has spectacular radio-emitting jets, fueled by a supermassive black hole deep within the galaxy's nucleus.

name: Antennae Galaxies (C60/61)
description: The two merging spiral galaxies that comprise the Antennae galaxies, Caldwell 60 and 61, began their interaction only a few hundred million years ago. Over the course of the merger, billions of stars will be formed.

name: Einstein Ring SDSS J0946+1006
description: Einstein rings like this form when two galaxies are almost perfectly aligned, one behind the other, and the gravitational field of the closer galaxy bends the light from the more distant galaxy into bright arcs around itself.

name: Galaxy NGC 2787
description: Galaxy NGC 2787 is located 24 million light-years from Earth in the constellation Ursa Major. Arms of dark dust encircle the galaxy's bright center. The points of light scattered around the galaxy are huge collections of old stars known as globuar clusters.

name: Star Cluster M92
description: This Hubble image of Messier 92's core captures the heart of a globular cluster — a ball of stars that orbits our galaxy's center like a satellite. The cluster, also called NGC 6341, is very tightly packed with stars, containing roughly 330,000 in total.

name: Quasar MC2 1635+119
description: This image shows shells of stars around a quasar known as MC2 1635+119. Quasars are among the brightest objects in the universe. They reside in the centers of galaxies and are powered by supermassive black holes.

name: Galaxy ESO 510-G13
description: Usually, when a spiral galaxy appears edge-on, its dust and spiral arms appear flat. The warping of the disk in ESO 510-G13 suggests this galaxy has recently undergone a collision with a nearby galaxy and is in the process of swallowing it.

name: Galaxy NGC 4993
description: In this galaxy, called NGC 4993, two neutron stars collided, creating gravitational waves discovered in 2017. The event produced a flash of light, called a kilonova, which appears to the upper left of center.

name: Galaxy Cluster RDCS 1252.9-2927
description: This image captures a massive cluster of galaxies called RDCS 1252.9-2927. This galaxy cluster existed when the universe was only 5 billion years old, or about 35 percent of its present age.

name: Galaxy C53
description: Lacking spiral arms but boasting a galactic bulge and prominent disk, lenticular galaxies like Caldwell 53, or NGC 3115, are crosses between the more familiar spiral and elliptical galaxies. Caldwell 53 also hosts a supermassive black hole roughly one billion times the mass of our Sun.

name: Galaxies Arp 142
description: The interacting galaxy duo near the bottom of this image is Arp 142. The pair contains the disturbed, star-forming spiral galaxy NGC 2936 along with its elliptical companion NGC 2937 to the left. To the right is an unrelated, bluish galaxy called UGC 5130.

name: Galaxy C48
description: Caldwell 48, also known as NGC 2775, is a spiral galaxy. The galaxy's large, yellowish, central bulge is filled with old stars, encircled by tightly wound spiral arms decorated by dark dust and clusters of young, blue stars.

name: Galaxy C7
description: Spiral galaxy Caldwell 7, also called NGC 2403, contains pinkish, glowing clouds filled with hot areas of ionized hydrogen, which can form thousands of stars over a couple million years. The  bright “star” in the upper right corner of this image is actually a supernova that was observed in 2004.

name: Galaxy NGC 6503
description: Most galaxies are clumped together in groups or clusters. A neighboring galaxy is never far away. But this galaxy, known as NGC 6503, has found itself in a lonely position, at the edge of a strangely empty patch of space called the Local Void.

name: Galaxies Arp 81
description: Arp 81 is a pair of interacting galaxies consisting of NGC 6621 (center) and NGC 6622 (left). The encounter has pulled a long tail out of NGC 6621 that has now wrapped behind the pair.

name: Galaxy Cluster Abell 2261
description: The giant elliptical galaxy in the center of this image is the most massive and brightest member of galaxy cluster Abell 2261. More than a million light-years wide, the galaxy is about 10 times bigger than our Milky Way galaxy.

name: Galaxy Hercules A
description: At the center of this image sits a large galaxy called Hercules A that harbors a supermassive black hole more than a thousand times as massive as the one in the Milky Way's center. Radio observations reveal large jets shooting away from the galaxy's core.

name: Star Cluster M12
description: Globular clusters like Messier 12 have high concentrations of gravitationally bound stars, so they often contain binary systems — two stars orbiting around each other. Some of these systems give off X-rays, and many have been detected in M12, also called NGC 6218.

name: Star Cluster C66
description: Caldwell 66, also known as NGC 5694, is one of the oldest, most remote globular clusters in our galaxy. It is far enough away from the galactic center, and traveling through space so quickly, that it will likely escape our galaxy.

name: Southern Pinwheel Galaxy (M83)
description: This image of the Southern Pinwheel spiral galaxy, also called Messier 83 or NGC 5236, captures thousands of star clusters, hundreds of thousands of individual stars, and "ghosts" of dead stars called supernova remnants.

name: Galaxies NGC 3314
description: This image shows a pair of galaxies called NGC 3314. Through a chance alignment, a face-on spiral galaxy lies precisely in front of another, larger spiral. This provides a view of dark material within the front galaxy, seen because it is silhouetted against the galaxy behind it.

name: Star Cluster C25
description: Unlike most of our Milky Way's globular clusters, which are huge, spherical clouds of old stars that formed with the galaxy, Caldwell 25 (NGC 2419) may not be from around here. Scientists surmise that the cluster may be the remains of a small galaxy that was captured and disrupted by the Milky Way.

name: Star Cluster C1
description: Caldwell 1, also known as NGC 188, is an open cluster, a group of stars that were all formed from the same large cloud of gas and are therefore all roughly the same age. One of the oldest open clusters known, Caldwell 1 is estimated to be at least 6.8 billion years old.

name: Star Cluster M10
description: The stars in globular clusters like Messier 10, or NGC 6254, are thought to have formed together, so they should all be roughly the same age. Yet M10 contains many stars that appear to be far younger than their neighbors. These stars were created either by collisions or interactions between stars.

name: Star Cluster M107
description: Messier 107, or NGC 6171, is one of approximately 150 globular star clusters found around the disk of the Milky Way galaxy. These spherical collections each contain hundreds of thousands of extremely old stars and are among the oldest objects in the Milky Way.

name: Star Cluster M80
description: This stellar swarm is Messier 80, one of the densest of the approximately 150 known globular clusters in the Milky Way galaxy. The cluster, also called NGC 6093, contains hundreds of thousands of stars held together by gravity.

name: Galaxy Centaurus A  (C77)
description: This image captures a turbulent firestorm of star birth along a nearly edge-on dust disk girdling nearby galaxy Centaurus A, also called Caldwell 77. Brilliant clusters of young, blue stars lie along the edge of the dark dust lane.

name: Galaxy NGC 4622
description: This image shows the spiral galaxy NGC 4622. Its outer pair of winding arms is full of new stars, clumped together in blue clusters. Strangely, the galaxy appears to be rotating clockwise, the opposite direction to what astronomers expected.

name: Nebula NGC 2371
description: This image captures the planetary nebula NGC 2371, the glowing remains of a Sun-like star. The remnant star visible at the center of NGC 2371 is the super-hot core of the former red giant, now stripped of its outer layers.

name: Galaxy Cluster MACS J0717.5+3745
description: Nearly every object in this image is a distant galaxy in the cluster MACS J0717.5+3745. Some faint arcs and streaks in the image are even farther galaxies whose light has been bent by the powerful gravity of the massive cluster.

name: Galaxy NGC 1569
description: The nearby dwarf galaxy NGC 1569 is a hotbed of vigorous star birth and is one of the closest "starburst" galaxies to us. The galaxy's "star factories" are manufacturing brilliant blue star clusters.

name: Iris Nebula (C4)
description: The Iris Nebula, also known as Caldwell 4 or NGC 7023, is a reflection nebula. Its color comes from the scattered light of its central star. Reflection nebulae are made of particles smaller than dust particles on Earth that diffuse light from nearby stars.

name: Fireworks Galaxy (C12)
description: The Fireworks galaxy (Caldwell 12 or NGC 6946) was named for its propensity for producing supernovae. This spiral galaxy's arms have been home to 10 observed supernovae since 1917. A tight, glowing ring around the galaxy's nucleus has likely been making stars for nearly 20 million years.

name: Blinking Planetary Nebula (C15)
description: The Blinking Planetary, also known as Caldwell 15 or NGC 6826, is a greenish orb of gas and dust, the outer layers of the burned-out star that jettisoned into space. The core of that star exists as a white dwarf at the center of the nebula.

name: Exoplanet HAT-P-7b
description: This is an artist's concept of the extrasolar planet HAT-P-7b, orbiting a star that is much hotter than our Sun. Hubble's millionth science observation was trained on this planet to look for the presence of water vapor and to study the planet's atmospheric structure.

name: Galaxy NGC 6745
description: This image captures the collision of two galaxies. The larger spiral galaxy, NGC 6745, boasts an intact nucleus as it interacts with the smaller, passing galaxy that is nearly out of the frame to the lower right.

name: Star Cluster M14
description: This image captures a portion of globular cluster Messier 14, home to over 150,000 stars. M14, also called NGC 6402, is located 29,000 light-years away from Earth in the constellation Ophiuchus.

name: Star Cluster M4
description: Messier 4, or NGC 6121, is a huge, spherical collection of stars known as a globular cluster and, at just 5,500 light-years away, the closest globular cluster to Earth. Home to more than 100,000 stars, the cluster is predicted to contain up to 40,000 white dwarfs — the cores of ancient, dead stars.

name: Omega Centauri (C80)
description: This view shows stars at the heart of Omega Centauri or Caldwell 80, one of roughly 150 globular clusters in our Milky Way galaxy. The behemoth stellar grouping is the biggest and brightest globular cluster in the Milky Way, and one of the few that can be seen by the unaided eye.

name: Galaxy C83
description: Caldwell 83, or NGC 4945, is a barred spiral galaxy that appears edge-on. Our own Milky Way galaxy is thought to be a barred spiral.

name: Southern Ring Nebula (C74)
description: This image of the Southern Ring Nebula, or Caldwell 74, clearly shows two stars near the center of the nebula: a bright, white one, and a fainter companion to its upper right. The faint star is actually the star that has ejected the material that forms the nebula.

name: Nebula C39
description: This planetary nebula, Caldwell 39, contains the glowing remains of a dying Sun-like star. The bright, central region is material being blown away by the nebula's central star.

name: Hidden Galaxy (C5)
description: Spiral galaxy IC 342, also known as Caldwell 5, displays intertwined tendrils of glowing, rosy dust in arms that wrap around a blue core of hot gas and stars. Difficult to see from Earth because of thick cosmic gas, dark dust, and glowing stars in the way, it is nicknamed the Hidden Galaxy.

name: Bowtie Nebula (C2)
description: This image captures a portion of the Bowtie Nebula, also known as Caldwell 2 or NGC 40. A shell of gas expands outward from the nebula's dying central star to create a planetary nebula, while energy from the star causes the gas to glow.

name: Exoplanet Kepler-1625b
description: This artist's depiction shows a possible moon orbiting a planet outside our solar system. The Hubble and Kepler space telescopes gathered evidence of a moon potentially as large as Neptune, accompanying a planet several times more massive than Jupiter called Kepler-1625b.

name: Star Cluster M56
description: Messier 56 is a globular cluster, a group of stars tightly bound by gravity. M56, or NGC 6779, has relatively few elements heavier than hydrogen and helium, which is a sign that its stars were born early in the universe's history.

name: Little Ghost Nebula
description: The Little Ghost Nebula appears as a small, ghostly cloud surrounding a dying star. It is found in the constellation Ophiuchus between 2,000 and 5,000 light-years away.

name: Star Cluster M62
description: Made up of gravitationally bound stars, Messier 62, or NGC 6266, is one of the most irregularly shaped globular clusters in our galaxy. This might be because it is one of the closest globular clusters to the galactic center and is affected by galactic tidal forces.

name: Southern Crab Nebula
description: An aging red giant star is shedding its outer layers to produce the Southern Crab Nebula. The "legs" are likely to be the places where the outflowing material slams into surrounding gas and dust.

name: Star Cluster C84
description: The nearly spherical globular star cluster Caldwell 84, or NGC 5286, is thought to be left over from a dwarf galaxy that collided with the Milky Way 8-10 million years ago. The bright star in the upper left of the cluster is likely closer to us than the cluster is.

name: Star Cluster C79
description: This image features stars at the center of the glittering globular cluster Caldwell 79, or NGC 3201. Globular clusters are dense, gravitationally bound packs of stars that roam the fringes of the galaxy. Most of the Milky Way's globular clusters are estimated to be over 10 billion years old.

name: Nebula NGC 2818
description: The spectacular structure of the planetary nebula NGC 2818 contains the outer layers of a dying star that were expelled into interstellar space. Our own Sun will undergo a similar process, but not for another 5 billion years or so.

name: Bubble Nebula (C11)
description: An enormous bubble is being blown into space by a super-hot, massive star. The Bubble Nebula, or Caldwell 11, is roughly seven light-years across and is located 7,100 light-years away in the constellation Cassiopeia.

name: Protostar IRAS 20324+4057
description: This caterpillar-shaped knot, called IRAS 20324+4057, is a protostar that is in the process of growing from the dust and gas surrounding it. However, other bright stars are blasting ultraviolet radiation at this "wanna-be" star and sculpting the gas and dust into its long shape.

name: Necklace Nebula
description: The Necklace Nebula contains the glowing remains of an ordinary, Sun-like star shedding material at the end of its life. The nebula consists of a bright ring, measuring 12 trillion miles across, dotted with dense, bright knots of gas that resemble diamonds in a necklace.

name: Herbig-Haro 32
description: This is an example of a "Herbig-Haro object," which is formed when young stars eject jets of material back into interstellar space. These jets plow into the surrounding nebula, producing strong shock waves that heat the gas and cause it to glow.

name: Wild Duck Cluster (M11)
description: Known as the Wild Duck Cluster for the roughly V-shaped arrangement of its brightest stars, the open cluster Messier 11, also called NGC 6705, is a group of stars that are only loosely bound by gravity. M11's 2,900 stars will eventually drift apart.

name: Swan Nebula (M17)
description: This image captures a small region within the Swan Nebula, also called Messier 17 or NGC 6618, a hotbed of star formation. The wave-like patterns of gas have been sculpted and illuminated by a torrent of ultraviolet radiation from young, massive stars, which lie outside the picture to the upper left.

name: Arches Cluster
description: The Arches cluster is the densest known star cluster in our galaxy and resides 25,000 light-years away. In this rough-and-tumble region, huge clouds of gas collide to form behemoth stars.

name: Galactic Center
description: This infrared image of the center of our Milky Way galaxy reveals a population of massive stars and complex structures in the hot ionized gas that swirls around the galactic core.

name: Star Cluster C76
description: This image captures the shining stars in a portion of the open star cluster Caldwell 76, also known as NGC 6231. Open clusters are groups of stars loosely bound together by gravity.

name: Star Cluster C82
description: This open star cluster, Caldwell 82 (or NGC 6193), is home to about 30 stars. It includes two rare O-type stars, the most massive and luminous kind of stars known. O-type stars are very hot, exceeding 30,000 Kelvin. For comparison, our Sun is about 5,800 Kelvin.

name: Ant Nebula
description: The Ant Nebula displays intriguing symmetrical patterns in the lobes of gas being ejected from a dying Sun-like star at its center.

name: Nebula NGC 5189
description: The  knotty, filamentary structure of NGC 5189 formed as a dying star shed its outer layers. Interestingly, this planetary nebula has two nested structures tilted with respect to each other.

name: Coalsack Nebula (C99)
description: Visible here is a small region on the edge of the Coalsack Nebula, or Caldwell 99. Caldwell 99 is a dark nebula — a dense cloud of interstellar dust that completely blocks visible light from objects behind it. The object at center is a much smaller protoplanetary nebula, forming from a dying star.

name: Star HD 96755
description: This "first light" image taken by Hubble was released in May 1990 after the telescope's launch. The observation centers on a star in the open cluster NGC 3532 and was intended to assist in focusing the telescope.

name: Star Cluster Westerlund 2
description: This image captures a giant cluster of about 3,000 stars called Westerlund 2. The cluster resides inside a vibrant stellar breeding ground known as Gum 29, located 20,000 light-years away in the constellation Carina.

name: Star RS Puppis
description: The bright star RS Puppis is swaddled in a cocoon of reflective dust illuminated by the glittering star. The star is 10 times more massive than our Sun and is 200 times larger.

name: Nebula NGC 2440
description: Planetary nebula NGC 2440 is a relic of a star once like our Sun that has cast off its outer layers of gas, forming a colorful cocoon around the star's remaining core.

name: Caroline's Cluster (C58)
description: Caroline's Cluster, or Caldwell 58, is an open cluster — a group of stars loosely bound together by gravity. Also known as NGC 2360, the cluster was named after astronomer Caroline Herschel, who discovered it in 1783.

name: V838 Monocerotis
description: These images capture a light echo from the star V838 Monocerotis. After the star brightened temporarily, light from that eruption began propagating outward through a dusty cloud around the star. The light reflects or "echoes" off the dust and then travels to Earth.

name: Cone Nebula
description: The Cone Nebula is a star-forming region in the constellation Monoceros. This conical pillar stretches over seven light-years and is just a small portion of a much larger star-formation complex.

name: Monkey Head Nebula
description: This image reveals carved knots of gas and dust in a small portion of the Monkey Head Nebula. The nebula is a star-forming region that hosts dusky dust clouds silhouetted against glowing gas.

name: Little Dumbbell Nebula (M76)
description: The Little Dumbbell Nebula, also known as Messier 76, is a planetary nebula, an expanding shell of gas around an aging or dying star. It's also known as the Cork Nebula, the Barbell Nebula, as well as both NGC 650 and NGC 651. Only half the nebula appears in this Hubble image.

name: Bok Globules
description: The dark knots of gas and dust in this image are a type known as Bok globules, and they are absorbing light in the center of the nearby nebula and star-forming region called NGC 281.

name: Egg Nebula
description: In the Egg Nebula, shells of dust form concentric rings around an aging star, resembling the layers of an onion. A thick dust belt, running almost vertically through the center, blocks light from the central star while twin beams of light radiate from the star.

name: Star Cluster M71
description: Messier 71, also called NGC 6838, is puzzling. It has been classified as both an abnormally dense open star cluster and as an unusually loose globular cluster. Today, astronomers think M71 is a young, loosely concentrated globular cluster — one of the smallest of its kind.

name: Star Cluster M22
description: Messier 22, also called NGC 6656, is one of about 150 globular star clusters in the Milky Way. Located just 10,000 light-years away in the constellation Sagittarius, it is one of the closest globular clusters to Earth.

name: Star Cluster M69
description: Messier 69, also called NGC 6637, is one of the most metal-rich globular clusters ever observed. Its stars have over 10 times more iron than stars in other globular clusters ― dense, gravitationally bound groups of stars ― of the same age.

name: Star Cluster C81
description: Caldwell 81, or NGC 6352, is a loose globular star cluster located roughly 20,000 light-years from Earth. Globular clusters ― collections of stars held together by gravity ― are thought to be among the oldest objects in a galaxy; Caldwell 81's age is roughly 12 billion years.

name: Star Cluster C89
description: This shining collection of stars is part of open cluster Caldwell 89, also known as NGC 6087. Open clusters are groups of stars loosely bound together by gravity; this one consists of approximately 40 stars, the brightest of which (near the center here) is called S Normae.

name: Galaxy ESO 99-4
description: ESO 99-4 is a galaxy with a highly peculiar shape. It is probably the remnant of an earlier merger process that has deformed it, leaving the main body largely obscured by dark bands of dust.

name: Hourglass Nebula
description: The Hourglass Nebula has been formed by a dying Sun-like star shedding its outer layers of gas. One theory suggests that the hourglass shape is produced as a fast stellar wind encounters a slowly expanding cloud that is more dense near the star's equator than near its poles.

name: Exoplanet HD 189733b
description: This artist's depiction shows exoplanet HD 189733b. When the planet passed in front of its parent star, Hubble was able to take spectral “fingerprints” of the planet's atmosphere, detecting strong evidence of hazes in the atmosphere of an exoplanet for the first time.

name: Nebula C90
description: Caldwell 90, or NGC 2867, is a planetary nebula, formed in the late stages of the evolution of a Sun-like star. The star's outer layers of gas were ejected, and the star itself became a hot, dense white dwarf.

name: Star Cluster C71
description: Caldwell 71, or NGC 2477, is an open star cluster ― a loosely bound collection of stars. This image shows just a portion of Caldwell 71.

name: Star VY Canis Majoris
description: The hypergiant star VY Canis Majoris is surrounded by clouds of gas that it has cast off in a long series of outbursts. These eruptions have formed loops, arcs, and knots of material moving at various speeds and in many different directions.

name: Supergiant Star Betelgeuse
description: Hubble captured this first direct image of a star other than the Sun. Called Betelgeuse, or Alpha Orionis, the red supergiant has a huge ultraviolet atmosphere with a mysterious hot spot twice the diameter of the Earth's orbit and is at least 2,000 Kelvin hotter than the surface of the star.

name: Crab Nebula (M1)
description: At the center of the Crab Nebula, also called Messier 1 or NGC 1952, sits a stellar remnant called a neutron star that has about the same mass as the Sun compressed into a sphere only a few miles across. Spinning 30 times a second, the neutron star shoots out beams of energy that make it look like it's pulsating.

name: Galaxy Perseus A (C24)
description: Hubble was the first to resolve giant but delicate filaments of gas shaped by strong magnetic fields around Perseus A — an elliptical galaxy also known as Caldwell 24 and NGC 1275. The galaxy's central black hole generates unusually powerful X-ray and radio emission.

name: Galaxy C23
description: If we could travel across space and time to see our own galaxy from the outside looking in, it would probably look a lot like Caldwell 23. This spiral galaxy, also called NGC 891, has been used to learn more about the structure and evolution of spiral galaxies.

name: Dwarf Galaxy C18
description: Also known as NGC 185, dwarf galaxy Caldwell 18 is a member of the Local Group of galaxies. Clouds of dust appear near the galaxy's center, and a globular star cluster appears to the left. Caldwell 18 also has an active galactic nucleus that emits extreme radiation.

name: Blue Snowball Nebula (C22)
description: The Snowball Nebula or Blue Snowball Nebula, also known as Caldwell 22 and NGC 7662, is a planetary nebula. When a medium-mass star like our Sun runs out of fuel, its outer layers of gas puff away into space to form a planetary nebula, leaving a small white dwarf at the nebula's center.

name: Galaxies IRAS 19297-0406
description: This image shows a tumultuous collision between four galaxies located 1 billion light-years from Earth. The tangled-up galaxies, called IRAS 19297-0406, are crammed together in the center of the picture.

name: Star Cluster M70
description: Quarters are always tight in globular clusters like Messier 70, where gravity binds together hundreds of thousands of stars in a small region of space. M70, or NGC 6681, has undergone a core collapse, which means that even more stars than usual squeeze into the object's core.

name: Nebula C68
description: Reflection nebulae like Caldwell 68 are star-forming clouds of gas and dust that glow with the energy emitted from a hot, nearby star.

name: Star Cluster C78
description: The globular star cluster Caldwell 78, also known as NGC 6541, is a sphere-shaped grouping of stars tightly bound together by gravity. Globular clusters are thought to be some of the oldest objects in a galaxy.

name: Star Cluster C86
description: Globular cluster Caldwell 86, also called NGC 6397, is a swarm of hundreds of thousands of stars. The cluster is dated at a staggering 13.4 billion years old.

name: Star Cluster C107
description: Globular clusters are groups of stars bound together by gravity. The stars in globular cluster Caldwell 107 (or NGC 6101) are more loosely packed than those in other globular clusters, which usually have dense, compact cores.

name: Brown Dwarf CHXR 73 B
description: The bright spot at lower right is a suspected brown dwarf, an object bigger than a planet but smaller than a star. Named CHXR 73 B, it orbits a red dwarf star dubbed CHXR 73, which is much less massive than the Sun.

name: Star Cluster NGC 2808
description: This dense swarm of stars lies at the center of the globular star cluster NGC 2808. Of the about 150 known globular clusters in our Milky Way galaxy, NGC 2808 is one of the most massive, containing more than a million stars.

name: Star Cluster C96
description: This image captures some of the shimmering stars in the outskirts of the open star cluster Caldwell 96. Also known as NGC 2516, or the Southern Beehive, this swarm of stars is loosely bound together by gravity.

name: Galaxies NGC 2207 and IC 2163
description: This image shows two interacting galaxies. The larger and more massive galaxy on the left is NGC 2207, and the smaller one on the right is IC 2163. Strong tidal forces from NGC 2207 have distorted the shape of IC 2163.

name: Red Rectangle Nebula
description: This image reveals details of one of the most unusual nebulae known in our Milky Way. Cataloged as HD 44179, this nebula is more commonly called the "Red Rectangle" because of its unique shape and color.

name: Horsehead Nebula
description: The backlit wisps along the Horsehead Nebula's upper ridge are being illuminated by a young five-star system just off the top of this image, taken in infrared light. Harsh radiation from one of these bright stars is slowly evaporating the nebula.

name: Nebula IC 349
description: IC 349 is a reflection nebula in the Pleiades star cluster (often called the "Seven Sisters"). The eerie, wispy tendrils of an interstellar cloud are being destroyed by one of the brightest stars in the star cluster.

name: Galaxies Arp 273
description: Arp 273 is of a pair of interacting galaxies that form a shape resembling a rose. The larger of the spiral galaxies, known as UGC 1810, has a disk that is distorted by the gravitational pull of the galaxy below it, known as UGC 1813.

name: Galaxy M110
description: Messier 110 is an elliptical galaxy, which means that it has a smooth and nearly featureless structure. Elliptical galaxies like M110, also called NGC 205, do not have arms or regions of star formation. The stars in elliptical galaxies are often older than those in other galaxies.

name: Stephan's Quintet
description: This close-up shows four of the five galaxies that make up Stephan's Quintet. The image reveals bright, blue clusters of stars, born from the violent interactions between some of the member galaxies.

name: Star Cluster C42
description: Globular cluster Caldwell 42, also known as NGC 7006, is a relic of the Milky Way's earliest years. Born as the galaxy was forming, these clusters provide a glimpse backward in time and provide a hint of what the Milky Way was like billions of years ago.

name: Star Cluster C47
description: Globular cluster Caldwell 47, or NGC 6934, is made up of many thousands of stars held together by gravity. Star clusters like this one encircle our galaxy like bees buzzing around a hive, typically in the galaxy's halo.

name: Galaxy C57
description: More than 2 million light-years away lies the irregular dwarf galaxy Caldwell 57, or NGC 6822. This image, taken to study the galaxy's stellar populations, shows a field of stars in the galaxy's outskirts. The dark streak is an artifact of Hubble's detector.

name: Summer Rose Star (M55)
description: Messier 55 is a globular cluster, a spherical group of stars bound together by gravity. Also called the Summer Rose Star, or NGC 6809, it is relatively spread out for a globular cluster, and contains around 100,000 stars.

name: Nebula C109
description: Caldwell 109, or NGC 3195, resembles a phantom snowball. The glowing shroud of gas was shed by its central star in a final act before dying. Our own Sun is destined to meet a similar fate in about 6 billion years.

name: Bullet Cluster
description: This composite image shows the galaxy cluster 1E 0657-556, also known as the Bullet Cluster. This cluster was formed after the collision of two large clusters of galaxies. Red shows X-ray emission while blue indicates the presence of matter.

name: Star LL Orionis
description: Named for the crescent-shaped wave made by a ship as it moves through water, a bow shock can be created in space when streams of gas collide. This image captures the bow shock around the  star LL Orionis.

name: Triangulum Galaxy (M33)
description: This mosaic captures the nearby Triangulum galaxy, also called Messier 33 or NGC 598. Striking areas of star birth glow bright blue throughout the galaxy, particularly in beautiful nebulae of hot gas like star-forming region NGC 604 in the lower left.

name: Galaxy NGC 7457 Core
description: The core of galaxy NGC 7457, shown here at different contrast levels, was the subject of the first science paper produced from Hubble observations. The paper reported observations of the environment around a suspected black hole in the galaxy's center.

name: Exoplanet HD 209458b
description: This is an artist's impression of a gas-giant planet orbiting the yellow, Sun-like star HD 209458, 150 light-years from Earth. Hubble made the first direct detection of an atmosphere around an extrasolar planet with its observations of this exoplanet.

name: Star Cluster M15
description: These stars belong to the globular cluster M15, also called NGC 7078. Nestled among them is an astronomical oddity, the first planetary nebula found in a globular cluster.

name: Star Cluster M75
description: Messier 75 is a globular cluster, a dense group of stars tightly bound together by gravity. In total, there are about 400,000 stars in M75, also called NGC 6864.

name: Galaxy NGC 6782
description: This spiral galaxy, NGC 6782, exhibits tightly wound spiral arms and a spectacular, nearly circular bright ring surrounding its nucleus. The ring contains many recently formed hot stars.

name: Galaxy C101
description: Spiral galaxy Caldwell 101, also called NGC 6744, is one of the relatively few large spiral galaxies in the neighboring universe. Its yellowish core is dominated by the light from old, cool stars, and its spiral arms are littered with dust that gives birth to star formation.

name: Nebula NGC 2074
description: The nebula NGC 2074 is a firestorm of raw stellar creation, perhaps triggered by a nearby supernova explosion. It lies in a nearby galaxy called the Large Magellanic Cloud, about 170,000 light-years away.

name: Beta Pictoris Disk
description: In 1984, Beta Pictoris was the very first star discovered to be surrounded by a bright disk of light-scattering dust and debris. Planets are thought to form in such disks, and astronomers have discovered two planets orbiting Beta Pictoris.

name: Star Cluster M79
description: The globular star cluster Messier 79 is located 41,000 light-years from Earth. It contains about 150,000 stars packed into an area measuring only 118 light-years across. Its stars are some of the oldest in our galaxy.

name: Star Cluster M2
description: Messier 2, or NGC 7089, is a globular cluster, a spherical group of stars tightly bound together by gravity. With a diameter of roughly 175 light-years, and a population of 150,000 stars, M2 is one of the largest clusters of its kind and one of the oldest known in the Milky Way.

name: Saturn Nebula (C55)
description: The Saturn Nebula, also known as Caldwell 55 and NGC 7009, is made up of the outer gaseous layers of a dying star. When a medium-mass star exhausts its nuclear fuel, it will swell up and shrug off its outer layers until only its small, hot core (known as a white dwarf) remains.

name: Star Cluster M72
description: A globular cluster is an ancient, spherical collection of old stars that are packed closely together. The stars in the cluster orbit around a mutual center of gravity at the cluster's dense core. Messier 72, or NGC 6981, is one of around 150 globular clusters discovered in the Milky Way galaxy.

name: Hickson Compact Group 87
description: This troupe of galaxies, known as Hickson Compact Group 87, is performing an intricate dance orchestrated by the mutual gravitational forces acting between them. The small spiral near the center could either be a member or an unrelated background object.

name: Galaxies AM 2026-424
description: The colliding galaxies AM 2026-424 resemble a face. Each "eye" is the bright core of a galaxy, one of which slammed into another. The outline of the face is a ring of young blue stars. Other clumps of new stars form a nose and mouth.

name: Nebula NGC 1748
description: Extremely intense radiation from newly born, ultra-bright stars has blown a glowing, spherical bubble in the nebula NGC 1748. The average-looking star at the very center of the bubble is about 30 times more massive and almost 200,000 times brighter than our Sun.

name: Nebula N44C
description: These wispy clouds of glowing gas make up a nebula known as N44C. It is part of the larger N44 complex, which includes young, hot, massive stars, other nebulae, and a "superbubble" blown out by multiple supernova explosions.

name: Galaxy NGC 1672
description: NGC 1672 is a barred spiral galaxy. Its arms do not twist all the way to the galaxy's center but attach to the ends of a bar of stars that extends from the nucleus. Clusters of hot, young, blue stars form along the spiral arms, while surrounding clouds of hydrogen gas glow red.

name: Star Cluster C73
description: Globular cluster Caldwell 73, or NGC 1851, is known to contain stars of different ages and is encircled by a diffuse halo of stars. Although the origins of the halo and multiple star populations are unknown, one idea is that Caldwell 73 is a remnant of two clusters that collided.

name: Galaxy NGC 1808
description: This close-up view shows a hotbed of star formation at the center of spiral galaxy NGC 1808. In the image, older stars appear yellow and young stars are blue.

name: Galaxy NGC 1600
description: The huge elliptical galaxy NGC 1600 is located 209 million light-years from Earth. The black hole that lurks at the center of the galaxy is one of the most massive black holes ever detected and 10 times more massive than expected for a galaxy of its size.

name: Galaxies Arp 147
description: Arp 147 consists of a pair of interacting galaxies. The left-most galaxy in this image appears nearly edge-on to our line of sight and features a smooth ring of starlight. The right-most galaxy exhibits a clumpy, blue ring of intense star formation.

name: Phantom Galaxy (M74)
description: Messier 74, or NGC 628, is a grand-design spiral galaxy. Symmetrical spiral arms reach out from the galaxy's central nucleus and are traced by winding dust lanes. The arms are dotted with clusters of young, blue stars and pink regions of glowing, ionized hydrogen.

name: Galaxy Cluster Cl 0024+17
description: In this image of the galaxy cluster Cl 0024+17, blue streaks are images of very distant galaxies that are behind the cluster. The distant galaxies appear distorted because their light is bent and magnified by the cluster's gravity.

name: Little Sombrero Galaxy (C43)
description: Nicknamed the Little Sombrero, Caldwell 43 (or NGC 7814) is an edge-on galaxy featuring a bright central bulge, a thin disk full of dust, and a glowing halo of gas and stars that sprawls out into space.

name: Propeller Galaxy (C44)
description: In the Propeller galaxy, also known as Caldwell 44 and NGC 7479, the tightly wound arms of the barred spiral galaxy create a backward "S" as they spin in a counter-clockwise direction.

name: Star Cluster NGC 265
description: This image displays a cluster of stars called NGC 265. The cluster resides in the Small Magellanic Cloud, one of the small galaxies orbiting our Milky Way galaxy.

name: Galaxy NGC 1313
description: This image resolves stars in the center of the barred spiral galaxy NGC 1313. The galaxy is roughly 14 million light-years away in the constellation Reticulum.

name: Galaxy Cluster MACS J0416
description: MACS J0416.1-2403 is a cluster of galaxies whose gravity is so intense that it warps and magnifies the light of more-distant galaxies behind it, a phenomenon called gravitational lensing. Scientists used intracluster light (highlighted in blue) to study dark matter within the cluster.

name: Galaxy Cluster Abell 370
description: Galaxy cluster Abell 370 contains an astounding assortment of several hundred galaxies tied together by the pull of gravity. Entangled among the galaxies are arcs of blue light ― distorted images of remote galaxies behind the cluster that have been magnified by the galaxy cluster's gravity.

name: Galaxy NGC 7674
description: NGC 7674 is a spiral galaxy tilted nearly face-on to Earth. Faint streamers below and to the left of the galaxy have been created by gravitational interactions with companion galaxies.

name: Star Cluster M30
description: Messier 30, or NGC 7099, is a globular cluster, a group of stars bound tightly together by gravity. Although globular clusters are mainly populated by old stars, the density of the stellar swarm leads to collisions and interactions that can appear to  rejuvenate some stars.

name: Hickson Compact Group 90
description: These three galaxies, called NGC 7173 (middle left), NGC 7174 (middle right) and NGC 7176 (lower right), are part of Hickson Compact Group 90, named after astronomer Paul Hickson, who cataloged small groups of galaxies like this one.

name: Hubble Deep Field South
description: The Hubble Deep Field South showcases a dazzling gallery of 2,500 galaxies in a tiny bit of sky visible from the Southern Hemisphere. This image complements the original Hubble Deep Field, which aimed Hubble at a small patch of space near the Big Dipper in the Northern Hemisphere.

name: Star Cluster C87
description: Globular star cluster Caldwell 87, or NGC 1261, is a group of stars tightly bound together by gravity. In the past, astronomers thought that all the stars in a globular cluster had similar ages and similar chemical abundances. However, recent studies suggest that this might not be true.

name: Galaxy NGC 1512
description: The core of the barred spiral galaxy NGC 1512 is unique for its stunning 2,400-light-year-wide circle of infant star clusters, called a "circumnuclear" starburst ring. Starbursts are episodes of vigorous star formation.

name: Galaxy NGC 1427A
description: This image captures NGC 1427A, an irregular dwarf galaxy that is warped by the gravitational influence of its larger galactic neighbors in the Fornax galaxy cluster.

name: Galaxy Cluster RCS2 032727-132623
description: The light from a distant galaxy, nearly 10 billion light-years away, has been warped into arcs and streaks by the gravity of galaxy cluster RCS2 032727-132623. The cluster acts as a gravitational lens, bending and amplifying light from the background galaxy.

name: Galaxy C51
description: Caldwell 51, also known as IC 1613, is an irregular dwarf galaxy and a member of our Local Group of galaxies, which includes the Milky Way and Andromeda. This image shows a region near the center of the galaxy.

name: Galaxy NGC 7714
description: The disrupted galaxy NGC 7714 displays a striking smoke-ring-like structure. The golden loop is made of Sun-like stars that have been pulled deep into space, far from the galaxy's center, by the gravity of a nearby galaxy that lies just out of view.

name: TRAPPIST-1 Exoplanets
description: The TRAPPIST-1 system is a group of seven planets around a star 40 light-years away. Observations show them to be Earth-sized, and Hubble has investigated them to assess their atmospheres and determine whether they are terrestrial in nature.

name: Helix Nebula (C63)
description: The Helix Nebula, also known as Caldwell 63 and NGC 7293, is one of the closest planetary nebulae, made up of glowing gas around a dying, Sun-like star. The Helix looks like a bubble, but is actually more like a tunnel of gas pointing back toward the star.

name: Galaxy Cluster Abell S1063
description: This Hubble image shows a soft blue haze, called intracluster light, among galaxies in the Abell S1063 cluster. The stars producing this glow have been thrown out from their galaxies. These stars are no longer part of a galaxy but align themselves with the gravity of the overall cluster.

name: Galaxy ESO 239-2
description: ESO 239-2 is the result of a cosmic collision between galaxies that will eventually result in a larger "elliptical" galaxy. The intermediate stage captured here shows a galaxy with long tails of dust and gas that envelop the galaxy's core.

name: Galaxy Fornax A
description: The dust lanes and star clusters of this giant elliptical galaxy, known as Fornax A or NGC 1316, give evidence that the galaxy formed from a past merger of two gas-rich galaxies. It is also one of the strongest sources of radio emission in the sky.

name: Hubble Legacy Field
description: This Hubble image represents the largest, most comprehensive "history book" of galaxies in the universe. A combination of nearly 7,500 separate Hubble exposures, it represents 16 years' worth of observations.

name: Galaxy NGC 1052-DF2
description: This galaxy, NGC 1052-DF2, is so diffuse we can see right through it to view more distant galaxies located behind it. The unusual galaxy is also missing most, if not all, of its dark matter.

name: Fomalhaut b Dust Cloud
description: Hubble detected what astronomers initially thought was a planet orbiting just inside a ring of dust around the star Fomalhaut. Later evidence from Hubble showed the object, called Fomalhaut b, fading, indicating it was likely a cloud of dust from a collision between two bodies.

name: Galaxy C67
description: Barred spiral galaxy Caldwell 67, or NGC 1097, contains a lane of stars and dust that extends away from the bright center toward the lower left and upper right of this image. The galaxy's vast spiral arms stretch much farther beyond this close-up Hubble view, over 100,000 light-years through space.

name: Skull Nebula (C56)
description: This image captures a serene view of a portion of the ominously named Skull Nebula, or Caldwell 56. This planetary nebula formed when a medium-sized star reached the end of its life, shedding layers of gas and leaving behind a white dwarf.

name: Galaxy ESO 243-49
description: This edge-on galaxy, called ESO 243-49, appears to host a medium-sized black hole that might have come from a cannibalized dwarf galaxy. As massive as 20,000 Suns, the black hole lies above the galactic plane — an unusual location that suggests it originated somewhere else.

name: Galaxy Cluster Abell 2667
description: While looking at galaxy cluster Abell 2667, astronomers found an odd-looking spiral galaxy (in the upper left corner of the image) that is plowing through the cluster and being ripped apart by the galaxy cluster's gravitational field and harsh environment.

name: Galaxy Cluster Abell 2744
description: Located 3.5 billion light-years away, Abell 2744 contains several hundred galaxies and might be a pile-up of at least four smaller galaxy clusters. Abell 2744's strong gravitational field acts as a lens, brightening and magnifying the light of nearly 3,000 distant background galaxies.

name: Cartwheel Galaxy
description: The unusual appearance of the Cartwheel galaxy (right) was created by a nearly head-on collision with a smaller galaxy. Its spoke-like structures are wisps of material connecting the galaxy's nucleus to an outer ring of young stars.

name: Galaxy NGC 300
description: NGC 300 is a spiral galaxy similar to our own Milky Way galaxy. Some of the bright blue specks in this image are young, massive stars called blue supergiants, and they are among the brightest stars seen in spiral galaxies.

name: Needle's Eye Galaxy (C62)
description: The Needle's Eye galaxy, also known as Caldwell 62 and NGC 247, is a dwarf spiral galaxy. It was given its nickname because one end of it features a strange void of stars (not seen in this Hubble close-up of the galaxy's starry center).

name: Sculptor Galaxy (C65)
description: In the Sculptor galaxy, also called Caldwell 65 or NGC 253, dark dust clouds and scattered massive stars obscure much of the galaxy's detail. A starburst galaxy, it contains a vast number of rapidly forming stars.
